news_summary: Urban trees in the DRC are recognized as valuable natural carbon sinks, emphasizing their importance for climate mitigation efforts.
news_summary_fr: Les arbres urbains en RDC sont reconnus comme de précieux puits de carbone naturels, ce qui souligne leur importance dans les efforts d'atténuation du changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Los árboles urbanos de la República Democrática del Congo se consideran valiosos sumideros naturales de carbono, lo que pone de relieve su importancia para las iniciativas de mitigación del cambio climático.

articles.title: DRC: Urban trees are natural CO₂ stores that merit closer attention
articles.summary: Urban trees are not just landscaping features. They are natural carbon sinks, capable of offsetting human activity-related carbon dioxide (CO₂) emissions.
articles.content:
This article was first published on March 8, 2026, on www.greenafia.com. Global Voices republished the article as part of a partnership agreement with GreenAfia.

Trees planted in large cities are an integral part of the solution to climate change. Although traditionally considered less important than old-growth forests, an October 2025 study shows that they can play a major role in conservation efforts, sparking interest among scientists in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC).

A scientific study conducted in the Ituri Province of Bunia, northeastern DRC, proved that urban trees are not just landscaping features. They are natural carbon sinks, capable of offsetting a substantial proportion of human activity-related carbon dioxide (CO₂) emissions.

Cities as Carbon sinks

Unlike the much-studied natural forests, African urban areas are largely absent from climate policies, due to a lack of reliable data. To address this shortage, researchers from the University of Bunia inventoried 2,311 trees across 21 one-hectare plots in three communes of Bunia: Mbunya, Nyakasanza, and Shari.

Using non-destructive methods based on tree diameter, height, and wood density, the team estimated the aboveground biomass and carbon stock without felling a single tree.

Figures from this study speak for themselves: 1,759 tons of aboveground biomass stored by urban trees in Bunia; 8,795 tons of carbon sequestered, which is equivalent to 2,374 tons of CO₂ removed from the atmosphere.

On average, one urban tree in Bunia stores 380 kilograms of carbon, which is equivalent to approximately 124 kg of CO₂ absorbed. In one urban hectare, the average carbon stock is 47.6 tons, a figure comparable to that of some degraded forest areas.

In Bunia, if an urban tree can offset up to 124 kg of CO₂, it gains a carbon value of USD 1 to 4 on the voluntary carbon market (a carbon credit trading mechanism that enables businesses and individuals to offset their carbon footprint voluntarily), proving that DR Congolese cities can transform their trees into effective climate assets.

Not all species play the same role

One of the study’s major contributions was identifying the most effective species for carbon sequestration: Eucalyptus globulus: 61 percent of the stored carbon; Mangifera indica (mango trees): 14 percent; Persea americana (avocado trees): 9 percent; Grevillea robusta: 7 percent; and Senna siamea: 5 percent.

These findings show that the choice of species is important. Some species, due to their wood density and rapid growth, play a disproportionate climatic role in relation to their number.

This study changes a great deal, especially in urban policy development, where DR Congolese cities can now include urban trees as climate infrastructure, alongside roads and drainage systems. Planting or preserving some trees, therefore, becomes a measurable climate mitigation strategy.

In terms of land use and reforestation, this study provides a scientific basis for selecting the species to prioritize, avoiding decorative plantings with low carbon impact, and focusing urban greening programs on species with a strong climate performance.

Bunia could also attract the attention of policymakers within the climate funding framework. With local data, cities like Bunia can pursue urban carbon-credit pilot projects, climate-adaptation and mitigation funding, and improved ecological management of their green spaces.

This study shows how the fight against climate change isn’t solely in the great forests of the Congo Basin, but also in the streets, fields, schools, and urban districts where every tree counts. But most importantly, every species selection, every preservation or destruction policy has a measurable climate cost.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study demonstrating that urban trees in the DRC act as substantial carbon sinks, providing measurable climate benefits. It highlights the potential for urban trees to be integrated into climate policies and carbon markets, impacting urban planning and conservation efforts broadly. The detailed data and implications for policy and climate action give the story strong substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Urban trees in DRC cities store large amounts of carbon, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Trees in cities have often been overlooked compared to big forests when it comes to fighting climate change. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), little was known about how much carbon urban trees could store.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Bunia studied 2,311 trees in three parts of Bunia city, in northeastern DRC. Using measurements like tree size and wood density, they estimated how much carbon these trees hold without cutting any down. They found that these urban trees store almost 8,800 tons of carbon, which is the same as removing about 2,374 tons of CO₂ from the air.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows city trees in the DRC help fight climate change by acting as natural carbon sinks. Some tree types, like eucalyptus and mango, store more carbon than others. This means city planners can choose the best trees to plant for the biggest climate benefit. Also, the carbon stored by these trees could be traded in carbon markets, giving cities a new way to earn money for protecting their green spaces.

**What's next step** – Cities like Bunia may start using this information to include trees in their plans for climate protection. They could create projects to sell carbon credits and get funding for planting and caring for trees that store the most carbon. This approach could change how urban areas in the DRC manage their environment and contribute to fighting climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have shown that trees in DRC cities store significant amounts of carbon, opening new opportunities for urban climate action and green planning.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les arbres urbains des villes de RDC stockent de grandes quantités de carbone, révèle une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les arbres en ville ont souvent été négligés par rapport aux grandes forêts dans la lutte contre le changement climatique. En République démocratique du Congo (RDC), on savait peu de choses sur la quantité de carbone que les arbres urbains pouvaient stocker.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Bunia ont étudié 2 311 arbres dans trois quartiers de la ville de Bunia, dans le nord-est de la RDC. En mesurant la taille et la densité du bois des arbres, ils ont estimé la quantité de carbone stockée sans abattre un seul arbre. Ils ont découvert que ces arbres urbains stockent près de 8 800 tonnes de carbone, ce qui équivaut à retirer environ 2 374 tonnes de CO₂ de l’air.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que les arbres en ville en RDC contribuent à la lutte contre le changement climatique en agissant comme des puits naturels de carbone. Certains types d’arbres, comme l’eucalyptus et le manguier, stockent plus de carbone que d’autres. Cela signifie que les urbanistes peuvent choisir les meilleurs arbres à planter pour un impact climatique plus fort. De plus, le carbone stocké par ces arbres pourrait être vendu sur les marchés du carbone, offrant aux villes une nouvelle source de revenus pour protéger leurs espaces verts.

**Étape suivante** – Des villes comme Bunia pourraient utiliser ces informations pour intégrer les arbres dans leurs plans de protection climatique. Elles pourraient lancer des projets pour vendre des crédits carbone et obtenir des financements pour planter et entretenir les arbres qui stockent le plus de carbone. Cette approche pourrait changer la gestion de l’environnement urbain en RDC et renforcer la lutte contre le changement climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont démontré que les arbres des villes en RDC stockent une quantité importante de carbone, ouvrant de nouvelles opportunités pour l’action climatique urbaine et la planification verte.
articles.personality_title_es: Los árboles urbanos en ciudades de RDC almacenan grandes cantidades de carbono, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los árboles en las ciudades a menudo se han pasado por alto frente a los grandes bosques en la lucha contra el cambio climático. En la República Democrática del Congo (RDC), se sabía poco sobre cuánto carbono pueden almacenar los árboles urbanos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Bunia estudiaron 2,311 árboles en tres zonas de la ciudad de Bunia, en el noreste de la RDC. Usando medidas como el tamaño y la densidad de la madera, calcularon cuánto carbono almacenan estos árboles sin talarlos. Encontraron que almacenan casi 8,800 toneladas de carbono, lo que equivale a eliminar unas 2,374 toneladas de CO₂ del aire.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que los árboles urbanos en la RDC ayudan a combatir el cambio climático actuando como sumideros naturales de carbono. Algunos tipos de árboles, como el eucalipto y el mango, almacenan más carbono que otros. Esto significa que los planificadores pueden elegir los mejores árboles para plantar y lograr un mayor beneficio climático. Además, el carbono almacenado puede venderse en mercados de carbono, ofreciendo a las ciudades una nueva forma de obtener ingresos para proteger sus espacios verdes.

**Próximo paso** – Ciudades como Bunia podrían usar esta información para incluir árboles en sus planes de protección climática. Podrían crear proyectos para vender créditos de carbono y obtener fondos para plantar y cuidar los árboles que almacenan más carbono. Esto podría cambiar la forma en que las áreas urbanas en la RDC manejan su medio ambiente y contribuyen a la lucha contra el cambio climático.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos demostraron que los árboles en ciudades de la RDC almacenan cantidades significativas de carbono, abriendo nuevas oportunidades para la acción climática urbana y la planificación verde.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant urban street scene in Bunia, DRC, where stylized, prominent eucalyptus, mango, avocado, grevillea, and senna trees with lush foliage are integrated harmoniously among simple buildings and pathways, their roots and branches subtly intertwining with glowing, abstract representations of carbon molecules being absorbed and stored, set against a soft, natural color palette evoking hope and environmental balance.
